problem_summary: 2022 MCM Problem C (data): starting with $1000 on 2016-09-11, build a model that uses ONLY the daily price stream up to each day to decide buy/hold/sell across a [Cash, Gold, Bitcoin] portfolio over a 5-year window (gold trades only on market days, bitcoin daily), with commissions (gold 1%, bitcoin 2%). Deliverables: (1) best daily strategy + final value of the $1000; (2) evidence the strategy is best; (3) sensitivity to transaction costs; (4) a 2-page memo. This team (Team #2203120) combined an ARIMA price-forecasting front-end with a parameterized dynamic-programming / threshold trading rule, reporting a final portfolio of $132,089.11.
models_used: ARIMA(p,d,q) rolling time-series price forecaster — gold ARIMA(2,1,1), bitcoin ARIMA(2,1,2); fit on 30-day windows, forecast next 3-day cycle, training window slides by 3 days
Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF) unit-root test for stationarity; first-order differencing (d=1) to stationarize (gold P 0.6734->0.0043, bitcoin 0.9921->0.0000)
ACF / PACF inspection to select AR order p and MA order q (truncation rule)
White-noise (residual) test on ARIMA residuals to confirm model adequacy
Risk-penalized dynamic-programming / sequential decision model: objective max_{w_j,m_j} A_j - lambda*S_j (total assets minus lambda times rolling 30-day asset standard deviation as risk term)
Threshold-based trade trigger rule using predicted vs. realized price change relative to increase/decrease thresholds e=beta*A, f=gamma*A
Decision coefficient R^2 (per-cycle fit) used to size the buy proportion (w_H when model stable, w_L otherwise); Hit-rate DS (directional accuracy) used as a gating constraint DS>=0.5
Inflation-comparison baseline model A = a*(1+r-I)^5 (r=0.5% deposit rate, I=7% inflation) to argue real-return superiority
Sensitivity metric defined as mean relative change in transaction frequency over 10 cost perturbations
key_techniques: Two-stage pipeline: forecast first (ARIMA), then feed the per-cycle prediction into a decision rule — cleanly separates prediction from policy

Cycle discretization: 3-day trading cycle with a 'cycle 0' (2016-09-11 to 2016-11-11) as a no-trade experience-accumulation/warm-up period, giving n=588 cycles — keeps the model strictly causal (only past data used)
Causal rolling-window training (30-day train / 3-day test, step 3) that mirrors the 'only data up to today' constraint of the problem
Risk modeled explicitly as standard deviation of recent total assets and folded into the objective via a regularization coefficient lambda (return-risk tradeoff in one scalar)

Parameterization as the core idea: a tunable parameter list (beta, gamma, w_H, w_L, m, q) turns ONE model into three trader personas (insurance / common / radical) — this directly answers 'best strategy' as a family rather than a point
Two-tier accuracy gating: directional hit-rate DS gates whether to trade at all; per-cycle R^2 sizes how aggressively to trade
Separate handling of gold vs bitcoin trading calendars (empty gold prediction = no-trade day)
Robustness shown by perturbing w_H, w_L, m and showing total assets stay flat (~$125k) across perturbations
Sensitivity-to-cost study with directional cases (cost up/down for gold/bitcoin separately) tying frequency, return, and risk changes together
why_it_won: "From a judge's view, the paper nails the three things Problem C rewards. (1) It respects the hard causality constraint — the warm-up cycle 0, rolling 30/3 windows, and per-cycle forecasting make it visibly 'only past data,' which many teams violate. (2) It reframes 'prove your strategy is best' — an unfalsifiable ask — into a tractable, defensible argument built on three pillars (generalization via the three trader types, robustness via parameter perturbation, and yield via an inflation/bank-deposit baseline showing $1000 grows to ~$132k vs a $139 real bank value). (3) Its parameterized single-model-many-personas design is elegant and reusable, and it produces concrete deliverables judges can check (final value, transaction counts, [C,G,B] end states). The pipeline is simple, transparent, and explainable, the writing is well-structured with per-problem framing, real cited literature, validation metrics (R^2 0.86/0.93, ADF p-values, hit rate), and a clean two-page memo that translates math into trader language. The combination of correct constraint handling + a clear evidentiary structure for 'optimality' + strong communication is what carries it to Outstanding."
section_highlights: "Summary block gives per-problem results up front (final $132,089.11; three-persona returns $14,312 / $50,040 / $132,089; cost sensitivities gold 0.0422 vs bitcoin 0.0658). Problem Analysis explicitly justifies model choice (ARIMA over LSTM/RNN/BP because endogenous-only, simple, efficient) — judges like seeing alternatives rejected with reasons. ARIMA section is a textbook-clean walkthrough: ADF stationarity table, ACF/PACF order selection, white-noise residual check, then validation with R^2. The strategy section lays out the objective max A - lambda*S with full s.t. constraints (non-negativity, DS>=0.5, cash floor C>=A*q, fixed commissions). Two decision flowcharts (Fig 6 'whether to trade', Fig 7 'how much to trade') make the policy auditable. Problem 2 structures the 'best' proof into generalization/robustness/yield with a results table (transactions, SD, R^2 per persona) and an inflation baseline. Problem 3 defines a clean sensitivity metric and uses directional cost cases. The memo restates the strategy as when/what/how-much to trade in plain language. Notation table and explicit assumptions appear early."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The 'dynamic programming' label is loose — there is no real Bellman recursion / value-function optimization over states; it is a greedy per-cycle threshold rule with a risk-penalized objective. A rigorous judge could dock this mislabeling.

Heavy ARIMA forecasting math is somewhat decorative: actual trades trigger on threshold comparisons of predicted vs realized change, so the elaborate model selection does less work than its page count suggests.

The objective max A_j - lambda*S_j is never actually optimized via a solver — lambda and the trade proportions are hand-set/tuned, so 'optimal' is asserted, not solved. Best-strategy claim rests on the three-pillar argument, not a proof of optimality.

Severe in-sample overfitting / look-ahead risk: parameters (beta, gamma, w_H, q, lambda) are tuned on the same 2016-2021 path whose return they report; no out-of-sample/walk-forward holdout validates the headline $132k.

Several equations are internally inconsistent or typo-ridden (e.g. Eq. 7-8 define B_i in terms of G_{i-1}; R^2 formula in Eq. 3 looks malformed; w_H/w_L appear swapped in Eqs. 14-15), suggesting the written model does not exactly match the code.
Robustness figures are suspiciously flat (every perturbation gives ~$125,000), which reads as under-resolved plotting rather than genuine evidence of stability.
Inflation baseline formula a*(1+r-I)^5 is economically crude and the $139 figure is used rhetorically; comparison to bank deposit is a weak benchmark vs buy-and-hold gold/bitcoin.
No transaction-cost-aware optimization of trade size beyond a fixed threshold; mixed gold+bitcoin purchase in one cycle admitted as not handled (stated in Improvements).
Authors self-disclose limited finance knowledge; risk is proxied only by asset-value standard deviation, ignoring drawdown, Sharpe, etc.
reusable_patterns: Forecast-then-decide two-stage architecture: a causal rolling-window time-series forecaster feeding a rule/optimization policy — generic template for any 'decide each day from past data' problem.
Warm-up / experience-accumulation period (cycle 0 with no action) to honor strict causality and avoid cold-start look-ahead — reusable whenever 'use only data up to t' is a hard constraint.

Single parameterized model -> multiple personas: expose a small parameter list and instantiate conservative/balanced/aggressive variants to answer 'find the best strategy' as a tunable family rather than one number.

Three-pillar 'prove it's best' rhetorical structure: generalization (parameter sweeps make different valid strategies), robustness (perturb parameters, show output stays bounded), and yield (beat a transparent baseline). Directly transplantable to any 'show your model is optimal/robust' sub-question.
Risk-penalized scalar objective: optimize value - lambda*(volatility proxy) so a single knob trades off return vs risk and generates the persona spectrum.
Two gating signals: a directional-accuracy gate (hit rate DS) to decide whether to act, plus a fit-quality signal (per-window R^2) to decide how strongly to act.
Sensitivity defined as mean relative change of an output (here trade frequency) across N perturbations of an input — a clean, reportable sensitivity number for the cost sub-question.
Standard ARIMA validation choreography (ADF -> difference -> ACF/PACF order pick -> white-noise residual check -> R^2/hit-rate report) as a ready checklist for any time-series MCM problem.
Closing memo that recasts the model as when/what/how-much decisions in stakeholder language — strong template for the required communication deliverable.
